title: The Cloud of Unknowing — The Via Negativa as Śāmbhavopāya
subtitle: The cloud of unknowing as the direct encounter with the absolute beyond all tattvas; the rejection of conceptual knowledge as the method of Śāmbhavopāya

summary.one_line: The Cloud of Unknowing read as a complete manual of Śāmbhavopāya: the via negativa as the direct method of the absolute, the cloud of forgetting as the release of the impure tattvas, and the blind ...
summary.scope: Focuses on the cloud of unknowing as the encounter with the absolute beyond all tattvas, the rejection of conceptual knowledge (vikalpa) as the method of Śāmbhavopāya, the 'blind stirring of love' (icchā) as the only means, the cloud of forgetting beneath as the release of the impure tattvas, and the soul as the spark/bindu of consciousness.

sources.label: The Cloud of Unknowing (anon., c. 1375–1400) — Spearing ed. (2001) or Walsh trans. (1981)
Evelyn Underhill — 'The Mystic Way' (1913) and introduction to The Cloud of Unknowing (1912 ed.)
Denys Turner — 'The Darkness of God: Negativity in Christian Mysticism' (1995)

body.text: [The 'cloud of unknowing' that must be entered is the direct encounter with the absolute beyond all tattvas — the Śuddhavidyā that is no object of knowledge but the very ground of knowing. The Cloud author insists that 'by the work of the understanding' nothing can be known of God — 'by love may He be gotten and holden; by thought never.' This is precisely the teaching of Śāmbhavopāya: the absolute is not reached by progressive purification of the tattvas (which is the lower upāyas) but by the immediate leap into the cloud that is not-knowing.]

[The Cloud's rejection of conceptual knowledge — 'put down all knowing under the cloud of forgetting' — is the rejection of vikalpa (discursive thought, conceptual construction). The author tells the disciple to strike down every thought, no matter how holy or sublime, and cover it with the cloud of forgetting. This is not the path of purification of thought (which would be Śāktopāya) but the abolition of thought itself — the direct method of Śāmbhavopāya, where the mind is not refined but dissolved.]

[The 'blind stirring of love' (blynde steryng of love) is icchā — the pure will, the fundamental desire of the soul for its source. This stirring is not an emotion but the very pulse of consciousness (spanda) before it differentiates into subject and object. The Cloud author describes it as 'a subtle stirring of love, coming from the pure delight of the spirit' — this is the spanda of the absolute, the primal vibration that is the only means of contact with the cloud. The stirring itself is the upāya.]

[The 'cloud of forgetting' that must be placed beneath the soul is the release of the impure tattvas — all the content of the 31 lower tattvas must be forgotten, covered, released. The Cloud author writes: 'forget all creatures that God ever made, and all their works — yea, so that your thought or desire be not directed to any of them.' This is not suppression but transcendence: the cloud of forgetting is the soul's refusal to identify with anything in the field of the impure, so that it may enter the cloud of unknowing — the pure path.]

[The 'naked intent' (nakyd entent) directed toward the cloud of unknowing is the purified consciousness — the bindu of awareness that has been stripped of all tattvic content. The Cloud author calls it 'a naked intent stretching unto God, and reaching only into the place of the cloud.' This is the bindu of Kashmir Shaivism: the point of concentrated consciousness that is the seed of all manifestation yet remains itself unmanifest. The naked intent is the soul reduced to its essence — the pure subjectivity that is the threshold of Śuddhavidyā.]

[The comparison of the soul to a spark — 'a little spark of the soul' (a litil spark of the soule) that can reach God — is the bindu of consciousness in its most concentrated form. The Cloud author speaks of the soul's 'point' (poynte) at which it touches the divine: 'the soul is a point that must be opened to the inspiration of the Holy Ghost.' This is the suṣumṇā piercing through the tattvas, the bindu that is both the soul in its contracted state (the impure) and the soul in its expanded state (the pure). The spark is the point at which the impure and pure meet — the threshold itself, where the daimon stands guard.]

gaps: Primary passages from the Cloud need extraction with Middle English quotations; comparative readings with Denys the Areopagite (the Cloud's main source), with the Via Negativa in the Upaniṣads, and with the Pratyabhijñā tradition of Kashmir Shaivism

issues.title: All passages currently reference the analytical framework rather than primary text
issues.description: This RO is currently a meta-index of the interpretive framework. Real passages from the Cloud need extraction as proper kind:source passages.

versions.summary: Initial RO created for The Cloud of Unknowing as Śāmbhavopāya manual. Six passages covering the cloud, vikalpa rejection, blind stirring, forgetting, naked intent, and the soul as spark.

timeline.event: RO created in draft state with analytical framework for the Cloud of Unknowing as Śāmbhavopāya
